review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer questioned the necessity of a separate MEMORY_HOTREMOVE config option, noting that it's only used in conjunction with MIGRATION and some scattered CONFIG_MEMORY_HOTREMOVE instances, and suggested removing it.
review_comments.raw_body:
On a long cardrive today I was wondering: do we still need a separate
MEMORY_HOTREMOVE

Now we'll have

config MEMORY_HOTREMOVE
	bool "Allow for memory hot remove"
	select HAVE_BOOTMEM_INFO_NODE if (X86_64 || PPC64)
	depends on MEMORY_HOTPLUG
	select MIGRATION

I'll try to get rid of that HAVE_BOOTMEM_INFO_NODE shite next.

Then it's really just MIGRATION and some sprinkled
CONFIG_MEMORY_HOTREMOVE. Not a lot of code.

-- 
Cheers,

David

review_comments.summary: Reviewer David (Arm) expressed uncertainty about removing CONFIG_ARCH_ENABLE_MEMORY_HOTREMOVE, citing that some architectures like s390x did not support HOTREMOVE in the past and questioned whether anyone would want to build a kernel with HOTPLUG but without the overhead for unplug+migration.

Then it made sense. I'm not sure if someone would want to build a kernel

So I would guess nothing major speaks against it.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3] firmware_loader: allow firmware_class.path to take multiple paths

version_history.subject: [PATCH] firmware_loader: allow firmware_class.path to take multiple paths
[PATCH v2] firmware_loader: allow firmware_class.path to take multiple paths
[PATCH v3] firmware_loader: allow firmware_class.path to take multiple paths

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch allows the firmware_class.path to accept multiple paths separated by a colon (:). It introduces a new helper function fw_try_firmware_path() that tries each path in order before falling back to default search paths. The patch also adds support for escaping literal colons and backslashes using a backslash ("). This change enables users to specify custom firmware directories via the kernel command line, improving flexibility and usability.

review_comments.author: Greg Kroah-Hartman
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Greg Kroah-Hartman expressed strong disagreement with the patch, questioning its necessity and suggesting a simpler approach using an array of paths instead of parsing escape codes.
review_comments.raw_body:
This is a mess, what could go wrong embedding another parser in the
kernel :)

Let's step back, why is this needed at all?  The kernel already supports
multiple standard locations for firmware paths, and one custom location.
Why do we now need more than that?  What changed to require this and who
is going to use it (and support it, and actually test it?)

We really want to make the number of firmware paths less, not more.  But
if we _really_ need multiple paths, isn't it simpler to just have an
array of paths here, not be forced to parse escape codes like you are
now doing?

thanks,

greg k-h

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author is addressing a request from an internal user for the ability to specify multiple firmware directories via firmware_class.path, and will investigate further to understand their use case.

Author responded to a question about how to pass multiple paths to the firmware_class.path module parameter, suggesting that delimiting them is necessary
review_comments.raw_body:
We have at least one internal user that requested the ability to do
this. I'll see if they can articulate their use-case better.

---

How would one feed an array of paths to the module without delimiting
them in some fashion?
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: Greg Kroah-Hartman
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Greg Kroah-Hartman expressed concerns about increasing complexity when attempting to escape special characters, suggesting that the approach may not be a good idea.
review_comments.raw_body:
I don't know, but once you start attempting to "escape" things, the
complexity goes up a lot and makes me think that this all isn't a good
idea :)

thanks,

greg k-h

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledges that the patch's escaping mechanism is limited but prefers it over adding multiple path slots, no fix planned
review_comments.raw_body:
Fair point, but the escaping here is pretty tame -- It's only escaping
':' and '\'. For better or worse, UNIX has very few characters that are
not allowed to be embedded in filenames, so our options for doing this
are somewhat limited.

If we really don't want to deal with escaping, then another option
might be to add a fixed number of path strings "slots" as parameters
(i.e. path1=/foo,path2=/bar,path3=...).

That seems uglier and more limiting than dealing with escapes though.
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: Greg Kroah-Hartman
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Greg Kroah-Hartman suggested revisiting the historical decision to limit firmware_class.path to a single path, expressing uncertainty about past changes and encouraging further investigation.
review_comments.raw_body:
For some reason, I thought we used to have more than one "path" that
userspace could pick for this, but we got rid of them all to just use
one.  So you might want to dig through the past history of this feature
to see why we did that?

thanks,

greg k-h

review_comments.author: Michal Grzedzicki
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the patch would allow multiple paths to be passed via firmware_class.path, which would simplify the distribution of firmware binaries as read-only snapshots and eliminate the need for a symlink farm. They also mentioned that this feature could make vendor shell scripts safer by allowing them to temporarily set firmware_class.path to the current working directory without worrying about restoring it afterwards.
review_comments.raw_body:
Internally, we distribute firmware binaries as read-only snapshots, and a system may have more than one. Without this feature, we must maintain a symlink farm to merge multiple snapshots into a single path. We would love to remove this workaround and switch to just passing multiple paths.

Also a common pattern I seen in vendor shell scripts is temporarily setting firmware_class.path to the current working directory, loading the module, and then restoring firmware_class.path to its previous value, with this feature it could be made safer.

Thanks,
Michal Grzedzicki

review_comments.author: Greg Kroah-Hartman
review_comments.summary: Reviewer questioned the safety of allowing multiple paths in firmware_class.path and suggested renaming the option to clearly indicate its new behavior.
review_comments.raw_body:
How would putting a bunch of different filepaths be "safer"?  A shell
script will have to read the value, append their path to it, and then
write it back?  And how will that ever work given that they will not
know if this is a kernel version that can support multiple paths in a
single value?

I think if you are going to change the way the option works, it needs to
be renamed so that everyone "knows" they can rely on it, otherwise no
one will use it.

thanks,

greg k-h

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author responded to Greg Kroah-Hartman's feedback, agreeing that the patch is safer because it doesn't require a shell script to manipulate the path string.

Author acknowledged a valid point about adding a separate module option for multiple paths, and is open to suggestions on the naming convention.
review_comments.raw_body:
It's safer in that you don't have to have a shell script monkeying with
the path string.

---

That is a valid point though. I'd be open to adding a separate module
option for this. Some name ideas:

paths=
searchpath=
search=

Anyone have opinions?
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

series_items.subject: [PATCH 00/14] nfsd/sunrpc: add support for netlink upcalls for mountd/exportd
series_items.patch_summary: This patchset adds support for netlink upcalls to the mountd/exportd services, replacing the outdated /proc-based interfaces used by these services. The new netlink-based interfaces provide a more modern and flexible way to handle cache notifications between the kernel and userland. This change is necessary due to limitations in the current implementation, including difficulty extending it to support new export options and reliance on /proc which may not be desirable in container environments. The patchset includes 14 patches that modify various kernel components, including nfsd, sunrpc, and netlink, to implement the new interfaces.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about the unused 'struct nfsd_genl_rqstp' definition in nfsctl.c, explaining that it's used elsewhere and providing updated code to reflect its usage.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about the naming consistency of upcall functions by renaming sunrpc_cache_pipe_upcall() to sunrpc_cache_upcall(), and updating all calls to it in various files.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern that the function name sunrpc_cache_pipe_upcall_timeout() was misleading, as it didn't actually handle timeouts. The author agreed to rename it to sunrpc_cache_upcall_warn(), which warns if there are no listeners.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern that the netlink upcalls would not handle both /proc and netlink interfaces, but explained that the new cache_do_upcall() function will handle both cases.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the missing cache_notify operation in struct cache_detail, agreeing to add it in a later patch and providing the necessary code changes.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about exposing the internal cache_request structure by adding two new functions, sunrpc_cache_requests_count() and sunrpc_cache_requests_snapshot(), which allow callers to count and snapshot pending upcall requests without exposing the struct cache_request. The author confirmed that these functions will be used by the nfsd generic netlink dumpit handler for svc_export upcall requests.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern that the auth.unix.ip and auth.unix.gid caches, being part of the sunrpc module, cannot use the nfsd generic netlink family. The author created a new 'sunrpc' generic netlink family with its own multicast group to support cache upcall notifications for these caches.
review_comments.raw_body:
The auth.unix.ip and auth.unix.gid caches live in the sunrpc module,
so they cannot use the nfsd generic netlink family. Create a new
"sunrpc" generic netlink family with its own "exportd" multicast
group to support cache upcall notifications for sunrpc-resident
caches.

Define a YAML spec (sunrpc_cache.yaml) with a cache-type enum
(ip_map, unix_gid), a cache-notify multicast event, and the
corresponding uapi header.

Implement sunrpc_cache_notify() which mirrors the nfsd_cache_notify()
pattern: check for listeners on the exportd multicast group, build
and send SUNRPC_CMD_CACHE_NOTIFY with the cache-type attribute.

Register/unregister the sunrpc_nl_family in init_sunrpc() and
cleanup_sunrpc().

Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
---
 Documentation/netlink/specs/sunrpc_cache.yaml | 40 ++++++++++++++++
 fs/nfsd/netlink.c                             |  2 +-
 include/uapi/linux/sunrpc_netlink.h           | 35 ++++++++++++++
 net/sunrpc/Makefile                           |  2 +-
 net/sunrpc/netlink.c                          | 66 +++++++++++++++++++++++++++
 net/sunrpc/netlink.h                          | 25 ++++++++++
 net/sunrpc/sunrpc_syms.c                      | 10 ++++
 7 files changed, 178 insertions(+), 2 deletions(-)

diff --git a/Documentation/netlink/specs/sunrpc_cache.yaml b/Documentation/netlink/specs/sunrpc_cache.yaml
new file mode 100644
index 0000000000000000000000000000000000000000..f4aa699598bca9ce0215bbc418d9ddcae25c0110
--- /dev/null
+++ b/Documentation/netlink/specs/sunrpc_cache.yaml
@@ -0,0 +1,40 @@
+# SPDX-License-Identifier: ((GPL-2.0 WITH Linux-syscall-note) OR BSD-3-Clause)
+---
+name: sunrpc
+protocol: genetlink
+uapi-header: linux/sunrpc_netlink.h
+
+doc: SUNRPC cache upcall support over generic netlink.
+
+definitions:
+  -
+    type: flags
+    name: cache-type
+    entries: [ip_map, unix_gid]
+
+attribute-sets:
+  -
+    name: cache-notify
+    attributes:
+      -
+        name: cache-type
+        type: u32
+        enum: cache-type
+
+operations:
+  list:
+    -
+      name: cache-notify
+      doc: Notification that there are cache requests that need servicing
+      attribute-set: cache-notify
+      mcgrp: exportd
+      event:
+        attributes:
+          - cache-type
+
+mcast-groups:
+  list:
+    -
+      name: none
+    -
+      name: exportd

diff --git a/net/sunrpc/netlink.c b/net/sunrpc/netlink.c
new file mode 100644
index 0000000000000000000000000000000000000000..e59ee82dfab358fc367045d9f04c394000c812ec
--- /dev/null
+++ b/net/sunrpc/netlink.c
@@ -0,0 +1,66 @@
+// SPDX-License-Identifier: ((GPL-2.0 WITH Linux-syscall-note) OR BSD-3-Clause)
+/* Do not edit directly, auto-generated from: */
+/*	Documentation/netlink/specs/sunrpc_cache.yaml */
+/* YNL-GEN kernel source */
+/* To regenerate run: tools/net/ynl/ynl-regen.sh */
+
+#include <net/netlink.h>
+#include <net/genetlink.h>
+#include <linux/sunrpc/cache.h>
+
+#include "netlink.h"
+
+#include <uapi/linux/sunrpc_netlink.h>
+
+/* Ops table for sunrpc */
+static const struct genl_split_ops sunrpc_nl_ops[] = {
+};
+
+static const struct genl_multicast_group sunrpc_nl_mcgrps[] = {
+	[SUNRPC_NLGRP_NONE] = { "none", },
+	[SUNRPC_NLGRP_EXPORTD] = { "exportd", },
+};
+
+struct genl_family sunrpc_nl_family __ro_after_init = {
+	.name		= SUNRPC_FAMILY_NAME,
+	.version	= SUNRPC_FAMILY_VERSION,
+	.netnsok	= true,
+	.parallel_ops	= true,
+	.module		= THIS_MODULE,
+	.split_ops	= sunrpc_nl_ops,
+	.n_split_ops	= ARRAY_SIZE(sunrpc_nl_ops),
+	.mcgrps		= sunrpc_nl_mcgrps,
+	.n_mcgrps	= ARRAY_SIZE(sunrpc_nl_mcgrps),
+};
+
+int sunrpc_cache_notify(struct cache_detail *cd, struct cache_head *h,
+			u32 cache_type)
+{
+	struct genlmsghdr *hdr;
+	struct sk_buff *msg;
+
+	if (!genl_has_listeners(&sunrpc_nl_family, cd->net,
+				SUNRPC_NLGRP_EXPORTD))
+		return -ENOLINK;
+
+	msg = genlmsg_new(GENLMSG_DEFAULT_SIZE, GFP_KERNEL);
+	if (!msg)
+		return -ENOMEM;
+
+	hdr = genlmsg_put(msg, 0, 0, &sunrpc_nl_family, 0,
+			  SUNRPC_CMD_CACHE_NOTIFY);
+	if (!hdr) {
+		nlmsg_free(msg);
+		return -ENOMEM;
+	}
+
+	if (nla_put_u32(msg, SUNRPC_A_CACHE_NOTIFY_CACHE_TYPE, cache_type)) {
+		nlmsg_free(msg);
+		return -ENOMEM;
+	}
+
+	genlmsg_end(msg, hdr);
+	return genlmsg_multicast_netns(&sunrpc_nl_family, cd->net, msg, 0,
+				       SUNRPC_NLGRP_EXPORTD, GFP_KERNEL);
+}
+EXPORT_SYMBOL_GPL(sunrpc_cache_notify);

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the ip_map cache upcall support, explaining that they added netlink-based interfaces for handling the sunrpc cache upcalls and implemented necessary helpers to count and snapshot pending cache requests.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the implementation of netlink upcalls for the unix_gid cache, specifically the handling of pending requests and responses from userspace. The author implemented new functions to snapshot pending requests and send them over netlink, as well as parse user-space responses and update the cache accordingly. The changes include updates to the sunrpc_cache YAML spec, generated headers, and kernel code.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Chuck Lever noted that a block comment above a code change would become outdated due to subsequent changes in the patch series.
review_comments.raw_body:
Nit: The block comment just above still reads "queue it up for read() by
the upcall daemon", which is will be made stale (or at least a little
misleading) later in the series.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Chuck Lever suggested that adding the new netlink upcall for mountd/exportd might be premature and should be moved to a subsequent patch in the series.

Reviewer noted that when a thread returns -EAGAIN from cache_do_upcall(), the CACHE_PENDING bit is already cleared by another thread, causing a netlink notification for an entry that is no longer pending, and suggested dropping the spinlock before calling wake_up() to avoid this issue.
review_comments.raw_body:
Nit: Adding this here might be premature. Should it be moved to a
subsequent patch in this series?

---

When ret == -EAGAIN, the CACHE_PENDING bit was already cleared by
another thread that won the race. Calling cache_notify in this case
sends a netlink notification for an entry that is no longer pending. The
winning thread already queued the request and will trigger its own
notification. If you agree this observation is not a false positive, a
possible fix might be:

  spin_unlock(&detail->queue_lock);
  if (ret != -EAGAIN && detail->cache_notify)
      detail->cache_notify(detail, h);
  wake_up(&detail->queue_wait);


-- 
Chuck Lever

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the sunrpc_cache_requests_count() function returns a count of pending cache requests, but this count can become stale as soon as the queue_lock is dropped, leading to incomplete data being returned to userspace in one-shot dumpit handlers.
review_comments.raw_body:
This API architecture introduces a TOCTOU, since as soon as the
queue_lock is dropped, the count can immediately become stale.

The count returned by sunrpc_cache_requests_count() is used as the array
bound. To wit:

  cnt = sunrpc_cache_requests_count(cd);

  items = kcalloc(cnt, sizeof(*items), GFP_KERNEL);

  seqnos = kcalloc(cnt, sizeof(*seqnos), GFP_KERNEL);

  cnt = sunrpc_cache_requests_snapshot(cd, items, seqnos, cnt);



This appears in all four dumpit handlers (ip_map, unix_gid, svc_export,
expkey).

This isn't a memory safety issue; _snapshot() caps its output at max, so
if the list grows between the two calls, entries are silently truncated
rather than overflowing the buffer. If the list shrinks, the arrays are
merely oversized.

However, the practical risk is incomplete data returned to userspace. If
the caller is guaranteed to be re-invoked (e.g., the netlink dumpit
callback gets called again until it returns 0), then missing items due
to list growth between count() and snapshot() is harmless: they'll be
picked up on the next pass.

But looking at the callers, they all do this:

  if (cb->args[0])
      return 0;

and then set cb->args[0] after the single snapshot dump.

The dumpit is a one-shot: it snapshots once and signals completion. If
the list grows between count() and snapshot(), the truncated entries are
silently lost and there's no subsequent pass to pick them up, IIUC.


-- 
Chuck Lever

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed Chuck Lever's concern about a potential race condition between the kernel and userland, explaining that userland will receive separate notifications for each queued cache request, ensuring it can pick up any remaining entries on subsequent passes.
review_comments.raw_body:
Userland will receive a separate notify request whenever a
cache_request is queued, and that notification is only sent after the
cache_request is queued.

So while it might not receive all of the queued requests from the
kernel in the race you describe, it won't matter because select() will
return the notify descriptor again on the next pass and mountd will
pick up the remaining entries at that point.
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

series_items.subject: [PATCH 06/14] sunrpc: add helpers to count and snapshot pending cache requests
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds two new functions, sunrpc_cache_requests_count() and sunrpc_cache_requests_snapshot(), to the Linux kernel's sunrpc module. These functions allow callers to count and snapshot the pending upcall request list without exposing internal data structures. The count function returns the number of requests with CACHE_PENDING set, while the snapshot function takes a reference on each item so it can be safely used after the queue lock is released. This patch is part of a larger series and will be used by the nfsd generic netlink dumpit handler for svc_export upcall requests.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 07/14] sunrpc: add a generic netlink family for cache upcalls
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a generic netlink family called 'sunrpc' to support cache upcall notifications for sunrpc-resident caches. The new family is defined in a YAML spec and includes a multicast group called 'exportd'. A function, `sunrpc_cache_notify()`, is implemented to mirror the pattern of `nfsd_cache_notify()` and sends SUNRPC_CMD_CACHE_NOTIFY with the cache-type attribute. The patch also registers and unregisters the sunrpc_nl_family in init_sunrpc() and cleanup_sunrpc(). This change allows for cache upcall notifications for sunrpc-resident caches, which were previously not supported.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 08/14] sunrpc: add netlink upcall for the auth.unix.ip cache
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds netlink-based cache upcall support for the ip_map (auth.unix.ip) cache in the sunrpc subsystem. It introduces new attributes and operations to the sunrpc_cache YAML spec, including ip-map attribute-set and ip-map-reqs wrapper. The patch also implements two new functions: one to snapshot pending requests and send them over netlink, and another to parse responses from userspace and update the cache. Additionally, it wires up an event notification for cache misses, triggering SUNRPC_CMD_CACHE_NOTIFY multicast events with SUNRPC_CACHE_TYPE_IP_MAP.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 09/14] sunrpc: add netlink upcall for the auth.unix.gid cache
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for netlink-based cache upcalls for the unix_gid (auth.unix.gid) cache in the sunrpc subsystem. It introduces new attributes and operations to the sunrpc_cache YAML spec, including a new attribute-set for unix-gid and operations for dumping pending requests and responding to user-space requests. The patch also implements callback functions to handle cache misses and multicast events. This change aims to improve the efficiency of cache management in the sunrpc subsystem by leveraging netlink-based upcalls.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 10/14] nfsd: add new netlink spec for svc_export upcall
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new netlink specification for the svc_export upcall in the NFS daemon (nfsd). The new spec includes definitions for flags, attributes, and attribute sets related to NFS exports, such as cache types, export flags, and security modes. It also introduces new attribute sets for file system locations, authentication flavors, and service export requests. The patch modifies several files, including netlink specs, C code, and header files, to implement the new specification.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 11/14] nfsd: add netlink upcall for the svc_export cache
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds netlink-based cache upcall support for the svc_export (nfsd.export) cache in the Linux kernel's NFS server component. It introduces a new callback function, nfsd_cache_notify(), which sends a multicast event to userspace when there are pending requests in the cache. The patch also implements two new functions: nfsd_nl_svc_export_get_reqs_dumpit() and nfsd_nl_svc_export_set_reqs_doit(). The first function dumps pending svc_export cache requests over netlink, while the second function parses responses from userspace and updates the cache accordingly. This allows for more efficient management of the svc_export cache and improves communication between the kernel and userspace.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 12/14] nfsd: add netlink upcall for the nfsd.fh cache
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds netlink-based cache upcall support for the NFS server's expkey (nfsd.fh) cache, allowing clients to notify the server of changes to cached file handles. The patch introduces a new attribute set and operations for expkey cache management, including dumping pending requests and updating the cache with client-provided information. It also wires up the expkey_notify callback to trigger multicast events when cache misses occur. This change follows the pattern established by existing svc_export netlink support and is intended to improve the efficiency of NFS server caching.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 13/14] sunrpc: add SUNRPC_CMD_CACHE_FLUSH netlink command
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new SUNRPC_CMD_CACHE_FLUSH generic netlink command that allows userspace to flush the sunrpc auth caches (ip_map and unix_gid) without writing to /proc/net/rpc/*/flush. The command is used by exportfs to replace its /proc-based cache_flush() with a netlink equivalent, providing a fallback to /proc for older kernels. An optional attribute selects which caches to flush, or all caches if omitted.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new netlink command, NFSD_CMD_CACHE_FLUSH, which allows userspace to flush the nfsd export caches without writing to /proc/net/rpc/*/flush. The command takes an optional attribute, NFSD_A_CACHE_FLUSH_MASK, that selects which caches to flush. If omitted, all nfsd caches are flushed. This is used by exportfs to replace its /proc-based cache_flush() with a netlink equivalent, with /proc fallback for older kernels.

review_comments.author: Michal Grzedzicki
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that without allowing multiple paths for firmware_class.path, systems must maintain a symlink farm to merge multiple snapshots into a single path, and suggested adding fixed slots for path strings as an alternative solution
review_comments.raw_body:
Internally, we distribute firmware binaries as read-only snapshots, and a system may have more than one. Without this feature, we must maintain a symlink farm to merge multiple snapshots into a single path. We would love to remove this workaround and switch to just passing multiple paths.

Also a common pattern I seen in vendor shell scripts is temporarily setting firmware_class.path to the current working directory, loading the module, and then restoring firmware_class.path to its previous value, with this feature it could be made safer.

Thanks,
Michal Grzedzicki

review_comments.summary: Reviewer questioned the safety of allowing multiple paths in a single value, citing potential issues with shell scripts appending and writing back to the value, and also expressed concern about compatibility with older kernel versions.
review_comments.raw_body:
How would putting a bunch of different filepaths be "safer"?  A shell
script will have to read the value, append their path to it, and then
write it back?  And how will that ever work given that they will not
know if this is a kernel version that can support multiple paths in a
single value?

I think if you are going to change the way the option works, it needs to
be renamed so that everyone "knows" they can rely on it, otherwise no
one will use it.

thanks,

greg k-h

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged a concern about escaping special characters in filenames, explained that the current escaping is limited to ':' and '\', and suggested adding multiple path slots as an alternative solution.
review_comments.raw_body:
Fair point, but the escaping here is pretty tame -- It's only escaping
':' and '\'. For better or worse, UNIX has very few characters that are
not allowed to be embedded in filenames, so our options for doing this
are somewhat limited.

If we really don't want to deal with escaping, then another option
might be to add a fixed number of path strings "slots" as parameters
(i.e. path1=/foo,path2=/bar,path3=...).

That seems uglier and more limiting than dealing with escapes though.
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: Michal Grzedzicki
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that without allowing multiple paths for firmware_class.path, systems must maintain a symlink farm to merge multiple snapshots into a single path, and suggested adding fixed slots for path strings as an alternative solution
review_comments.raw_body:
Internally, we distribute firmware binaries as read-only snapshots, and a system may have more than one. Without this feature, we must maintain a symlink farm to merge multiple snapshots into a single path. We would love to remove this workaround and switch to just passing multiple paths.

Also a common pattern I seen in vendor shell scripts is temporarily setting firmware_class.path to the current working directory, loading the module, and then restoring firmware_class.path to its previous value, with this feature it could be made safer.

Thanks,
Michal Grzedzicki

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 0/1] writeback: don't block sync(2) for filesystems with no data integrity guarantees

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 0/1] writeback: don't block sync(2) for filesystems with no data integrity guarantees

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 1/1] writeback: don't block sync(2) for filesystems with no data integrity guarantees
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new superblock flag, SB_I_NO_DATA_INTEGRITY, to indicate filesystems that cannot guarantee data persistence on sync. For such filesystems, the sync(2) system call will initiate writeback of dirty inodes but will not wait for the flusher threads to complete the writeback. This change is necessary to prevent hangs seen on fuse-overlayfs when the user session cgroups are frozen before invoking kernel suspend. The patch replaces a per-inode flag with a superblock-level flag, allowing for more efficient handling of data integrity guarantees.

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary:
The reviewer, David (Arm), agrees with the patch and finds it sensible.

Reviewer David suggested moving the handling of SB_I_NO_DATA_INTEGRITY superblock flag to sync_inodes_sb() or adding a warning to it, to catch existing and future callers.
review_comments.raw_body:
Makes sense to me.

[...]

---

I was wondering whether that handling should be moved to
sync_inodes_sb(), so it would catch any (existing+future) callers.

Alternatively, we could catch abuse by adding a warning to sync_inodes_sb.

-- 
Cheers,

David

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author addressed a concern about the potential for deadlock on fuse filesystems when sync(2) is called, explaining that the patch's behavior is consistent with the original implementation before tmp folios were removed and that the issue was already present in the previous code. The author does not indicate any plans to revise the patch.

Author agrees to restructure the code by bringing back a previously removed comment block, which will help clarify the logic for filesystems without data integrity guarantees.

Author acknowledged that the patch's approach to skipping writeback completion in sync(2) may not be consistent across all callers, and agreed to restructure the code in v3 to address this issue.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, Mar 19, 2026 at 2:26 PM David Hildenbrand (Arm)
<david@kernel.org> wrote:

---

That's a good idea - I'll bring back the comment block that was
previously in wait_sb_inodes() and move it here

---

Thinking about this some more, I think you're right.

At the vfs layer, the only other relevant callers are
generic_shutdown_super() when handling an unmount and syncfs for
syncing a specific fd. For these, I was thinking it'd be more useful
to return after writeback has actually completed, since the caller has
to directly invoke the operation on a specific fd/mount. But that's
wrong, we shouldn't be picking and choosing which syncs are ok vs not
ok to wait for. And I'm realizing now there's also the
hibernate/suspend call paths that call filesystems_freeze_callback()
which can also call into sync_filesystem() -> sync_inodes_sb()...

I'll make this change for v3.

Thanks,
Joanne

<david@kernel.org> wrote:

> > Add a SB_I_NO_DATA_INTEGRITY superblock flag for filesystems that cannot
> > guarantee data persistence on sync (eg fuse). For superblocks with this

> > commit f9a49aa302a0 ("fs/writeback: skip AS_NO_DATA_INTEGRITY mappings
> > in wait_sb_inodes()"). The flag belongs at the superblock level because
> > data integrity is a filesystem-wide property, not a per-inode one.
> > Having this flag at the superblock level allows us to skip the logic in
> > sync_inodes_sb() entirely, rather than iterating every dirty inode in

> >       if (sb->s_user_ns != &init_user_ns)

> >  {

>

That's a good idea - I'll bring back the comment block that was

> > +     if (sb->s_iflags & SB_I_NO_DATA_INTEGRITY)

Thinking about this some more, I think you're right.

At the vfs layer, the only other relevant callers are

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new superblock flag, SB_I_NO_DATA_INTEGRITY, to identify filesystems that cannot guarantee data persistence on sync operations. It then uses this flag to skip the inode writeback for these filesystems during sync(2) operations, improving efficiency and preventing potential hangs. The change replaces a per-inode flag with a superblock-level flag, as data integrity is a filesystem-wide property. This patch restores fuse's previous behavior before tmp folios were removed, where sync was essentially a no-op.

review_comments.author: Jan Kara
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that while sync(2) may not provide data integrity guarantees, it still flushed data for non-broken FUSE servers, and suggested kicking a flush worker to do writeback in the background for filesystems with SB_I_NO_DATA_INTEGRITY

I'd note that previously, if the FUSE server was not broken, although
sync(2) would not provide any data integrity guarantee, it would still
flush the data so practically, there would be no user observable difference
unless you really did powerfail testing. So some users might be
unpleasantly surprised by sync(2) suddently not doing anything on FUSE
filesystems. Maybe for SB_I_NO_DATA_INTEGRITY filesystems we should at
least kick flush worker to do writeback in the background?

The original function was identical in both cases, but had been duplicated due to historical reasons. This change simplifies the codebase and reduces maintenance overhead.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 5/7] mm: list_lru: introduce caller locking for additions and deletions
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces caller locking for additions and deletions in the list_lru API, allowing callers to synchronize auxiliary state with LRU state. The THP shrinker is a specific use case that requires this feature. Normal and irqsafe locking primitives are provided as well as caller-locked variants of addition and deletion functions. This change enables the THP shrinker to switch from its custom LRU to list_lru.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 6/7] mm: list_lru: introduce folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a helper function, folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc(), to allocate list_lru heads for memory control groups (memcgs) from folios. It aims to make the existence check lightweight under RCU and only pin the memcg when necessary. This is a preparatory step for switching the THP shrinker to use list_lru.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 7/7] mm: switch deferred split shrinker to list_lru
series_items.patch_summary: This patch switches the deferred split shrinker to use list_lru, which properly sets up per-node and per-cgroup lists. This improves handling of cgroups on NUMA systems by avoiding splitting large pages on other nodes when a node-restricted allocation is reclaiming memory. The change also streamlines list operations and reclaim walks, and is consistent with how other major shrinkers use list_lru.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series switches the THP (Transparent Huge Pages) shrinker to use list_lru instead of an open-coded deferred split queue. The current implementation has issues, including being non-NUMA-aware and complicated in its interactions with other code. Using list_lru fixes these problems and simplifies planned shrinker work. The patch series includes several cleanups and refactors in the list_lru code to make the conversion easier, as well as extensions to the list_lru API to allow for more control over locking scope and a convenience helper for allocating LRU heads when coming from a folio.

version_history.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: avoid GFP_ATOMIC allocations in qgroup free paths

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses a GFP_ATOMIC allocation issue in the btrfs qgroup free paths by introducing a new sentinel value (EXTENT_CHANGESET_BYTES_ONLY) to signal that only bytes_changed should be tracked, eliminating the need for GFP_ATOMIC allocations.

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary: Identified the patch as a good solution to the GFP_ATOMIC allocation issue, with no technical concerns or suggestions raised.
review_comments.raw_body:
在 2026/3/20 10:19, Leo Martins 写道:
> When qgroups are enabled, __btrfs_qgroup_release_data() and
> qgroup_free_reserved_data() pass an extent_changeset to
> btrfs_clear_record_extent_bits() to track how many bytes had their
> EXTENT_QGROUP_RESERVED bits cleared. Inside the extent IO tree spinlock,
> add_extent_changeset() calls ulist_add() with GFP_ATOMIC to record each
> changed range. If this allocation fails, it hits a BUG_ON and panics the
> kernel.
> 
> However, both of these callers only read changeset.bytes_changed
> afterwards — the range_changed ulist is populated and immediately freed
> without ever being iterated. The GFP_ATOMIC allocation is entirely
> unnecessary for these paths.
> 
> Introduce extent_changeset_init_bytes_only() which uses a sentinel value
> (EXTENT_CHANGESET_BYTES_ONLY) on the ulist's prealloc field to signal
> that only bytes_changed should be tracked. add_extent_changeset() checks
> for this sentinel and returns early after updating bytes_changed,
> skipping the ulist_add() call entirely. This eliminates the GFP_ATOMIC
> allocation and makes the BUG_ON unreachable for these paths.
> 
> Callers that need range tracking (qgroup_reserve_data,
> qgroup_unreserve_range, btrfs_qgroup_check_reserved_leak) continue to
> use extent_changeset_init() and are unaffected.
> 
> Signed-off-by: Leo Martins <loemra.dev@gmail.com>

Looks good to me.

Reviewed-by: Qu Wenruo <wqu@suse.com>

Thanks,
Qu

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 00/21] Virtual Swap Space
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series implements virtual swap space, a new approach to handling swap operations in Linux. The current method ties a swap entry to its backing slot, which can lead to performance issues when swap is used as a cache or optimized. This series introduces a cluster-based allocation algorithm for virtual swap slots, reducing lock contention and memory overhead. It also reuses the swap table for reverse mapping, removes unnecessary components like the swap cache and zswap tree, and reduces the size of the swap descriptor.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the swap cache being tightly coupled to physical swap infrastructure by announcing plans to decouple it and move it to the virtual swap layer, where it will be managed at the virtual swap layer.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about the organization and categorization of the swap API in include/linux/swap.h, promising to restructure it as part of a clean-up effort without any functional changes intended.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the swapoff path needs to drop the per-vswap spinlock before calling try_to_unmap(), agreed to restructure in v2, and provided a generic API abstraction for this behavior.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author addressed a concern about the zswap entry operations being tightly coupled to the physical swap layer by adding new helper functions to abstract away these operations, facilitating re-implementation when swap is virtualized.

Author addressed a concern about the swap_zswap_tree() function, which was previously using xa_store(), xa_load(), and xa_erase() macros to manipulate the xarray tree. The author replaced these macros with new inline functions zswap_entry_store(), zswap_entry_load(), and zswap_entry_erase() that directly call the corresponding xarray operations.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about the swapcached state being managed at the virtual swap layer by abstracting away a function in userfaultfd.c that directly looks at the swapfile's swap map to check if a swap entry is cached.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing a concern about the swap virtualization implementation, specifically the addition of new code for the vswap debugfs directory without any behavioral changes intended. The author has added scaffolds for the new code and set up vswap compilation in the Makefile, with no changes to existing behavior.
review_comments.raw_body:
In prepration for the implementation of swap virtualization, add new
scaffolds for the new code: a new mm/vswap.c source file, which
currently only holds the logic to set up the (for now, empty) vswap
debugfs directory. Hook this up in the swap setup step in
mm/swap_state.c, and set up vswap compilation in the Makefile.

Other than the debugfs directory, no behavioral change intended.

Finally, make Johannes a swap reviewer, given that he has contributed
majorly to the developments of virtual swap.

Signed-off-by: Nhat Pham <nphamcs@gmail.com>
---
 MAINTAINERS          |  2 ++
 include/linux/swap.h |  3 +++
 mm/Makefile          |  2 +-
 mm/swap_state.c      |  6 ++++++
 mm/vswap.c           | 35 +++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++
 5 files changed, 47 insertions(+), 1 deletion(-)
 create mode 100644 mm/vswap.c

diff --git a/MAINTAINERS b/MAINTAINERS
index e087673237636..b21038b160a07 100644
--- a/MAINTAINERS
+++ b/MAINTAINERS
@@ -16728,6 +16728,7 @@ R:	Kemeng Shi <shikemeng@huaweicloud.com>
 R:	Nhat Pham <nphamcs@gmail.com>
 R:	Baoquan He <bhe@redhat.com>
 R:	Barry Song <baohua@kernel.org>
+R:	Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>
 L:	linux-mm@kvack.org
 S:	Maintained
 F:	Documentation/mm/swap-table.rst
@@ -16740,6 +16741,7 @@ F:	mm/swap.h
 F:	mm/swap_table.h
 F:	mm/swap_state.c
 F:	mm/swapfile.c
+F:	mm/vswap.c
 
 MEMORY MANAGEMENT - THP (TRANSPARENT HUGE PAGE)
 M:	Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org>
diff --git a/include/linux/swap.h b/include/linux/swap.h
index 918b47da55f44..df0771903a952 100644
--- a/include/linux/swap.h
+++ b/include/linux/swap.h
@@ -423,6 +423,9 @@ extern void __meminit kswapd_stop(int nid);
 
 #ifdef CONFIG_SWAP
 
+/* Virtual swap space API (mm/vswap.c) */
+int vswap_init(void);
+
 /* Lifecycle swap API (mm/swapfile.c) */
 int folio_alloc_swap(struct folio *folio);
 bool folio_free_swap(struct folio *folio);
diff --git a/mm/Makefile b/mm/Makefile
index 2d0570a16e5be..67fa4586e7e18 100644
--- a/mm/Makefile
+++ b/mm/Makefile
@@ -75,7 +75,7 @@ ifdef CONFIG_MMU
 	obj-$(CONFIG_ADVISE_SYSCALLS)	+= madvise.o
 endif
 
-obj-$(CONFIG_SWAP)	+= page_io.o swap_state.o swapfile.o
+obj-$(CONFIG_SWAP)	+= page_io.o swap_state.o swapfile.o vswap.o
 obj-$(CONFIG_ZSWAP)	+= zswap.o
 obj-$(CONFIG_HAS_DMA)	+= dmapool.o
 obj-$(CONFIG_HUGETLBFS)	+= hugetlb.o hugetlb_sysfs.o hugetlb_sysctl.o
diff --git a/mm/swap_state.c b/mm/swap_state.c
index e2e9f55bea3bb..29ec666be4204 100644
--- a/mm/swap_state.c
+++ b/mm/swap_state.c
@@ -882,6 +882,12 @@ static int __init swap_init(void)
 	int err;
 	struct kobject *swap_kobj;
 
+	err = vswap_init();
+	if (err) {
+		pr_err("failed to initialize virtual swap space\n");
+		return err;
+	}
+
 	swap_kobj = kobject_create_and_add("swap", mm_kobj);
 	if (!swap_kobj) {
 		pr_err("failed to create swap kobject\n");
diff --git a/mm/vswap.c b/mm/vswap.c
new file mode 100644
index 0000000000000..e68234f053fc9
--- /dev/null
+++ b/mm/vswap.c
@@ -0,0 +1,35 @@
+// SPDX-License-Identifier: GPL-2.0
+/*
+ * Virtual swap space
+ *
+ * Copyright (C) 2024 Meta Platforms, Inc., Nhat Pham
+ */
+#include <linux/swap.h>
+
+#ifdef CONFIG_DEBUG_FS
+#include <linux/debugfs.h>
+
+static struct dentry *vswap_debugfs_root;
+
+static int vswap_debug_fs_init(void)
+{
+	if (!debugfs_initialized())
+		return -ENODEV;
+
+	vswap_debugfs_root = debugfs_create_dir("vswap", NULL);
+	return 0;
+}
+#else
+static int vswap_debug_fs_init(void)
+{
+	return 0;
+}
+#endif
+
+int vswap_init(void)
+{
+	if (vswap_debug_fs_init())
+		pr_warn("Failed to initialize vswap debugfs\n");
+
+	return 0;
+}

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 01/21] mm/swap: decouple swap cache from physical swap infrastructure
series_items.patch_summary: This patch decouples the swap cache from the physical swap infrastructure, preparing for virtualization of swap space. It removes swap cache related helpers from the swap table and repurposes the remaining infrastructure to serve as a rmap (physical -> virtual swap mapping). The patch also introduces a single global lock to synchronize swap cache accesses temporarily, which will be replaced by swap clusters once the swap cache is moved to the virtual swap layer.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 02/21] swap: rearrange the swap header file
series_items.patch_summary: This patch rearranges the swap header file (include/linux/swap.h) to group related functions into categories such as lifecycle swap, swap cache API, and physical swapfile allocator and device API. The functions are reorganized for better structure and clarity, but no functional changes are made. Extern keywords are removed from the functions that were rearranged.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 03/21] mm: swap: add an abstract API for locking out swapoff
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces an abstract API to lock out swapoff in the Linux kernel's memory management system. The current implementation of locking out swapoff is based on getting a reference to the backing swap device, which does not make sense in the new virtual swap design where swap entries may not have a backing swap device. This patch abstracts away the swapoff locking behavior into a generic API called `tryget_swap_entry` and `put_swap_entry`, allowing for more flexibility and decoupling of swap backends from swap entries. The changes are made to various kernel files, including `memory.c`, `mincore.c`, and others.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 04/21] zswap: add new helpers for zswap entry operations
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds new helper functions to abstract away zswap entry operations, making it easier to re-implement these functions when swap is virtualized. The new helpers include storing, loading, erasing, and checking if a zswap entry is empty. These functions use the xarray data structure to manage the zswap entries. The patch modifies several existing functions in the mm/zswap.c file to use the new helpers instead of directly accessing the xarray.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 05/21] mm/swap: add a new function to check if a swap entry is in swap cached.
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new function called is_swap_cached to the Linux kernel's mm/swap module. The function checks if a given swap entry is currently cached in memory, which is necessary for userfaultfd operations. To minimize overhead, it directly accesses the swap_map array using READ_ONCE. This abstraction will be useful when the virtual swap layer starts managing swap cache state.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 06/21] mm: swap: add a separate type for physical swap slots
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new type called swp_slot_t to represent physical swap slots on swap devices, separate from the existing swp_entry_t type which represents logical swap entries. The mapping between these two types is initially an identity mapping, but will be used in later patches to dynamically allocate virtual swap slots and associate them with backing stores.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 07/21] mm: create scaffolds for the new virtual swap implementation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch creates scaffolds for the new virtual swap implementation by adding a new mm/vswap.c source file, which currently only sets up an empty debugfs directory. It also hooks this up in the swap setup step and adds vswap compilation to the Makefile. Additionally, it makes Johannes Weiner a reviewer for virtual swap developments and updates the MAINTAINERS file accordingly.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 08/21] zswap: prepare zswap for swap virtualization
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prepares the zswap code for swap virtualization by removing its dependency on range partition logic and instead using a unified zswap tree. The changes include updating the zswap.h header file to remove unused functions, modifying the swapfile.c file to remove calls to these functions, and making similar changes to the zswap.c file. This patch is part of a larger series that aims to virtualize swap space in Linux.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 09/21] mm: swap: allocate a virtual swap slot for each swapped out page
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new virtual swap slot design, where each swapped out page is dynamically allocated a virtual swap slot and associated metadata structure. The virtual swap slot replaces the physical swap slot in page table entries and is used to index into swap data structures such as the swap cache and zswap tree. The patch also repurposes the swap table infrastructure as a reverse map to look up the virtual swap slot from its associated physical swap slot on the swapfile. This change improves the efficiency of swap operations, including cluster readahead and swap file reclamation.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 10/21] swap: move swap cache to virtual swap descriptor
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the swap cache and workingset shadow for anonymous pages to the virtual swap descriptor, effectively range-partitioning the swap cache by virtual swap clusters of PMD size. This change eliminates lock contention in the swap cache. The patch updates several kernel files, including huge_memory.c, migrate.c, shmem.c, and vmscan.c, to use the new swap cache locking mechanism. The approach taken is to associate each swap entry with its corresponding virtual swap descriptor, allowing for more efficient and concurrent access to the swap cache.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 11/21] zswap: move zswap entry management to the virtual swap descriptor
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the management of zswap entries from a dedicated tree to the virtual swap descriptor, eliminating lock contention between zswap and other swap operations. The zswap pool is re-partitioned by virtual swap cluster, allowing for more efficient use of memory. This change simplifies the code and improves performance by reducing synchronization overhead.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 12/21] swap: implement the swap_cgroup API using virtual swap
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the swap_cgroup API to use virtual swap, which allows for dynamic allocation of memory for swap entries' cgroup information. This reduces the memory overhead in large but sparsely used swap spaces by only allocating memory when needed. The patch removes the static array for storing swap entries' cgroup information and instead stores it in the swap descriptor. It also updates various files to reflect this change, including include/linux/swap_cgroup.h, mm/Makefile, and mm/vswap.c.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 13/21] swap: manage swap entry lifecycle at the virtual swap layer
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the management of swap entry lifecycle to the virtual swap layer, introducing two new fields in the swap descriptor: 'in_swapcache' and 'swap_count'. The existing swap entry lifecycle API is re-implemented in the virtual swap layer. This change simplifies the logic by not implementing swap count continuation for now.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 14/21] mm: swap: decouple virtual swap slot from backing store
series_items.patch_summary: This patch decouples virtual swap slots from their backing storage, allowing them to be backed by physical swap slots, zero swap pages, compressed objects in the zswap pool, or in-memory pages. This change enables the use of zswap and zero swap page optimization without reserving a slot on a swapfile or a swapfile at all, resulting in significant disk space savings for hosts with high memory usage. The patch also introduces new requirements for batched swap operations to ensure that entries have consistent backing states.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 15/21] zswap: do not start zswap shrinker if there is no physical swap slots
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prevents the zswap shrinker from starting if there are no physical swap slots available, which is the case when swap is virtualized and pre-allocated slots on the swapfile for each zswap entry do not exist.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 16/21] swap: do not unnecesarily pin readahead swap entries
series_items.patch_summary: This patch optimizes swap readahead by avoiding unnecessary pinning of swap entries in the same virtual swap cluster as the target entry. The patch introduces a new function `vswap_same_cluster` to check if two virtual swap entries belong to the same cluster, and uses this function to skip pinning swap entries that are already protected by the pinned target entry. This reduces overhead and improves performance.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 17/21] swapfile: remove zeromap bitmap
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes the zeromap bitmap from the virtual swap layer in Linux. The zeromap bitmap was used to track zero pages, but since zero swap entries are now treated as a separate backend, this bitmap is no longer needed. Removing it saves memory overhead and does not change any behavior.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 18/21] memcg: swap: only charge physical swap slots
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's memory cgroup (memcg) swap handling to only charge physical swap slots, rather than virtual swap slots. This change is made because zswap and zero-filled swap page optimization no longer occupy physical swap space. The patch introduces new functions for recording and clearing swap charges in memcgs, and updates existing code to use these new functions. The goal of this patch is to improve the accuracy of swap usage and limit calculations in memcgs.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 19/21] swap: simplify swapoff using virtual swap
series_items.patch_summary: This patch simplifies and optimizes the swapoff process by utilizing virtual swap slots stored at page table entries, eliminating the need for a page table walk. Instead of traversing all VMAs, it iterates through allocated swap slots on the swapfile, finds their corresponding virtual swap slots, and faults them in. This approach reduces kernel CPU time by about 13% compared to the original code.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 20/21] swapfile: replace the swap map with bitmaps
series_items.patch_summary: This patch replaces the swap map in Linux's swapfile code with two bitmaps, one for tracking allocated swap entries and another for bad entries. This change is made possible by moving the swap count state to the virtual swap layer, which reduces the number of states that need to be tracked from four to three. The replacement of the swap map with bitmaps saves 6 bits per swap entry, reducing memory usage. The patch updates the swap_info_struct struct in include/linux/swap.h and modifies several functions in mm/swapfile.c to work with the new bitmap-based representation.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch optimizes the vswap_free() function by batching contiguous free calls to avoid unnecessary lock drops and improve performance. The patch introduces a new function, mem_cgroup_id_put_many(), which allows for batched release of memory control group IDs. It also modifies the vswap_cluster_free() function to use this new function and reduces the number of lock acquisitions in vswap_free(). This change is particularly beneficial for non-disk-swap backends where lock drops are unnecessary, resulting in improved performance.

review_comments.raw_body:
Hmm seems like we can just defer-free on the cpu_dead path, and that
should be safe?

Right now, if a cluster is cached on a CPU, we know that it's not
cached on any other CPU, and it's not on any other partial lists.

enough of an event that we dont have a backlog of free deferrals :)

The other alternative is workqueue (with some careful rcu handling in
the free callback), but that seems unnecessary.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/4] arm64: request contpte-sized folios for exec memory
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series modifies the arm64 architecture to request contpte-sized folios for executable memory, optimizing iTLB entries and TLB coalescing. The current implementation uses a fixed order of 64K folios, which is suboptimal for 16K and 64K base pages. The proposed change uses ilog2(CONT_PTES) instead, directly evaluating to contpte-aligned order for all page sizes.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Expressed concern that the patch may default to using 2M folios on all page sizes, potentially leading to inefficient memory allocation. Suggested considering mapping vma size as well.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/4] mm: bypass mmap_miss heuristic for VM_EXEC readahead
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the behavior of the mmap_miss heuristic in the Linux kernel's memory management (mm) module. The mmap_miss counter tracks whether readahead is useful for mmap'd file access and disables all readahead, including targeted VM_EXEC readahead, when it exceeds a certain threshold. However, on arm64 with 64K base pages, the decrement paths are inactive due to specific conditions, causing the counter to monotonically increase past the threshold after 100 page faults. The patch fixes this issue by moving the VM_EXEC readahead block above the mmap_miss check, allowing targeted readahead for executable memory without being gated by the heuristic.

review_comments.author: Jan Kara
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Jan Kara noted that moving the VM_EXEC readahead logic earlier would disable VM_HUGEPAGE handling for VM_EXEC vmas, and instead suggested disabling the mmap_miss logic for VM_EXEC vmas by adding a conditional check in the if statement.
review_comments.raw_body:
I can see the problem but I'm not sure what you propose is the right fix.
If you move the VM_EXEC logic earlier, you'll effectively disable

shouldn't we rather disable mmap_miss logic for VM_EXEC vmas like:

	if (!(vm_flags & (VM_SEQ_READ | VM_EXEC))) {
		...
	}

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/4] elf: align ET_DYN base to exec folio order for contpte mapping
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adjusts the alignment of ELF binaries (ET_DYN) to ensure that their load address is aligned to the size of large folios allocated for execution memory. This is necessary because some architectures, such as arm64, require a specific alignment for contpte mapping to work correctly. Without this adjustment, the readahead patches cannot benefit from contpte mapping, leading to reduced iTLB coalescing benefits. The patch introduces a new calculation for ELF alignment based on the arch's requested folio size, ensuring that virtual addresses of exec pages are aligned to the folio boundary.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author is addressing a concern about the alignment of ET_DYN base to exec folio order for contpte mapping, specifically on ARM architecture where 64K page size is used. They explain that they chose to align with 2M (cont-pte size) to get both page fault benefit and iTLB coverage, which is already the case on x86.

The author is addressing a concern that the patch only checks for alignment when there's at least one PT_LOAD segment large enough to contain a full folio, and proposes to check all segments with PT_LOAD instead. The author agrees that this makes sense and suggests applying the change over the entire series.
review_comments.raw_body:
For us it came about because we use 64K page size on ARM, and none of the
text sections were getting hugified (because PMD size is 512M). I went with
exec_folio_order() = cont-pte size (2M) for 16K and 64K as we can get both page
fault benefit (which might not be that important) and iTLB coverage (due to
cont-pte).
x86 already faults in at 2M (HPAGE_PMD_ORDER) due to force_thp_readahead path in
do_sync_mmap_readahead() so the memory pressure introduced in ARM won't be worse
than what already exists in x86.

---

Yes I think this makes sense!

Although maybe we should check all segments with PT_LOAD. So maybe something
like below over this series?

diff --git a/fs/binfmt_elf.c b/fs/binfmt_elf.c
index 2d2b3e9fd474f..a0e83b541a7d8 100644
--- a/fs/binfmt_elf.c
+++ b/fs/binfmt_elf.c
@@ -1116,10 +1116,30 @@ static int load_elf_binary(struct linux_binprm *bprm)
                         * the hardware cannot coalesce PTEs (e.g. arm64
                         * contpte) even though the physical memory and
                         * file offset are correctly aligned.
+                        *
+                        * Only increase alignment when at least one
+                        * PT_LOAD segment is large enough to contain a
+                        * full folio and has its file offset and virtual
+                        * address folio-aligned. This avoids reducing
+                        * ASLR entropy for small binaries that cannot
+                        * benefit from contpte mapping.
                         */
-                       if (exec_folio_order())
-                               alignment = max(alignment,
-                                       (unsigned long)PAGE_SIZE << exec_folio_order());
+                       if (exec_folio_order()) {
+                               unsigned long folio_sz = PAGE_SIZE << exec_folio_order();
+
+                               for (i = 0; i < elf_ex->e_phnum; i++) {
+                                       if (elf_phdata[i].p_type != PT_LOAD)
+                                               continue;
+                                       if (elf_phdata[i].p_filesz < folio_sz)
+                                               continue;
+                                       if (!IS_ALIGNED(elf_phdata[i].p_vaddr, folio_sz))
+                                               continue;
+                                       if (!IS_ALIGNED(elf_phdata[i].p_offset, folio_sz))
+                                               continue;
+                                       alignment = max(alignment, folio_sz);
+                                       break;
+                               }
+                       }

series_items.subject: [PATCH 4/4] mm: align file-backed mmap to exec folio order in thp_get_unmapped_area
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series introduces a fallback alignment mechanism in the mm module to ensure file-backed mappings, particularly those for executable segments, can be contptemapped even when large folios are used. The approach involves adding a new alignment check in thp_get_unmapped_area_vmflags() that tries PAGE_SIZE << exec_folio_order() alignment when PMD_SIZE alignment fails.

review_comments.author: WANG Rui
review_comments.summary: Identified an issue where even when the code segment of a shared library satisfies PMD_SIZE, it still doesn't end up at a PMD-aligned virtual address. Suggested skipping the PMD_SIZE == folio_sz case to avoid calling __thp_get_unmapped_area() twice with the same parameters.
review_comments.raw_body:
> +	if (exec_folio_order()) {
> +		unsigned long folio_size = PAGE_SIZE << exec_folio_order();
> +
> +		ret = __thp_get_unmapped_area(filp, addr, len, off, flags,
> +					      folio_size, vm_flags);
> +		if (ret)
> +			return ret;
> +	}
> +

I noticed that even when the code segment of a user-space shared library
satisfies PMD_SIZE (32MB), it still doesn\u2019t end up at a PMD-aligned virtual
address. This might be the fallback you mentioned. Adjusting p_align in the
ld.so ELF loader does work, though it also avoids extremely large PMD_SIZE
values (capped at \u226432M).

It would probably be better to skip the PMD_SIZE == folio_sz case here,
so we don\u2019t end up calling __thp_get_unmapped_area() twice with the same
parameters.

Thanks,
Rui

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses issues with contpte-sized exec folios on arm64, specifically for 16K and 64K page configurations. The first issue is that the current implementation returns an incorrect order for these page sizes, which is fixed by using a different calculation. The second issue is that readahead is disabled after 100 page faults due to the mmap_miss heuristic, but this only happens on 64K pages because the decrement paths are inactive. This is fixed by moving the VM_EXEC readahead block above the mmap_miss check. Finally, the patch ensures that the virtual address is aligned to CONT_PTE_SIZE (2M) for contpte mapping, which requires alignment of the VMA start address.

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer David (Arm) questioned the assumption that a single order is sufficient for contpte mapping, suggesting that decisions should be based on the mapping size instead.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 3/10/26 15:51, Usama Arif wrote:
> On arm64, the contpte hardware feature coalesces multiple contiguous PTEs
> into a single iTLB entry, reducing iTLB pressure for large executable
> mappings.
> 
> exec_folio_order() was introduced [1] to request readahead at an
> arch-preferred folio order for executable memory, enabling contpte
> mapping on the fault path.
> 
> However, several things prevent this from working optimally on 16K and
> 64K page configurations:
> 
> 1. exec_folio_order() returns ilog2(SZ_64K >> PAGE_SHIFT), which only
>    produces the optimal contpte order for 4K pages. For 16K pages it
>    returns order 2 (64K) instead of order 7 (2M), and for 64K pages it
>    returns order 0 (64K) instead of order 5 (2M). Patch 1 fixes this by
>    using ilog2(CONT_PTES) which evaluates to the optimal order for all
>    page sizes.
> 
> 2. Even with the optimal order, the mmap_miss heuristic in
>    do_sync_mmap_readahead() silently disables exec readahead after 100
>    page faults. The mmap_miss counter tracks whether readahead is useful
>    for mmap'd file access:
> 
>    - Incremented by 1 in do_sync_mmap_readahead() on every page cache
>      miss (page needed IO).
> 
>    - Decremented by N in filemap_map_pages() for N pages successfully
>      mapped via fault-around (pages found in cache without faulting,
>      evidence that readahead was useful). Only non-workingset pages
>      count and recently evicted and re-read pages don't count as hits.
> 
>    - Decremented by 1 in do_async_mmap_readahead() when a PG_readahead
>      marker page is found (indicates sequential consumption of readahead
>      pages).
> 
>    When mmap_miss exceeds MMAP_LOTSAMISS (100), all readahead is
>    disabled. On 64K pages, both decrement paths are inactive:
> 
>    - filemap_map_pages() is never called because fault_around_pages
>      (65536 >> PAGE_SHIFT = 1) disables should_fault_around(), which
>      requires fault_around_pages > 1. With only 1 page in the
>      fault-around window, there is nothing "around" to map.
> 
>    - do_async_mmap_readahead() never fires for exec mappings because
>      exec readahead sets async_size = 0, so no PG_readahead markers
>      are placed.
> 
>    With no decrements, mmap_miss monotonically increases past
>    MMAP_LOTSAMISS after 100 faults, disabling exec readahead
>    for the remainder of the mapping.
>    Patch 2 fixes this by moving the VM_EXEC readahead block
>    above the mmap_miss check, since exec readahead is targeted (one
>    folio at the fault location, async_size=0) not speculative prefetch.
> 
> 3. Even with correct folio order and readahead, contpte mapping requires
>    the virtual address to be aligned to CONT_PTE_SIZE (2M on 64K pages).
>    The readahead path aligns file offsets and the buddy allocator aligns
>    physical memory, but the virtual address depends on the VMA start.
>    For PIE binaries, ASLR randomizes the load address at PAGE_SIZE (64K)
>    granularity, giving only a 1/32 chance of 2M alignment. When
>    misaligned, contpte_set_ptes() never sets the contiguous PTE bit for
>    any folio in the VMA, resulting in zero iTLB coalescing benefit.
> 
>    Patch 3 fixes this for the main binary by bumping the ELF loader's
>    alignment to PAGE_SIZE << exec_folio_order() for ET_DYN binaries.
> 
>    Patch 4 fixes this for shared libraries by adding a contpte-size
>    alignment fallback in thp_get_unmapped_area_vmflags(). The existing
>    PMD_SIZE alignment (512M on 64K pages) is too large for typical shared
>    libraries, so this smaller fallback (2M) succeeds where PMD fails.
> 
> I created a benchmark that mmaps a large executable file and calls
> RET-stub functions at PAGE_SIZE offsets across it. "Cold" measures
> fault + readahead cost. "Random" first faults in all pages with a
> sequential sweep (not measured), then measures time for calling random
> offsets, isolating iTLB miss cost for scattered execution.
> 
> The benchmark results on Neoverse V2 (Grace), arm64 with 64K base pages,
> 512MB executable file on ext4, averaged over 3 runs:
> 
>   Phase      | Baseline     | Patched      | Improvement
>   -----------|--------------|--------------|------------------
>   Cold fault | 83.4 ms      | 41.3 ms      | 50% faster
>   Random     | 76.0 ms      | 58.3 ms      | 23% faster

I'm curious: is a single order really what we want?

I'd instead assume that we might want to make decisions based on the
mapping size.

Assume you have a 128M mapping, wouldn't we want to use a different
alignment than, say, for a 1M mapping, a 128K mapping or a 8k mapping?

-- 
Cheers,

David

---

> exec_folio_order() was introduced [1] to request readahead of executable
> file-backed pages at an arch-preferred folio order, so that the hardware
> can coalesce contiguous PTEs into fewer iTLB entries (contpte).
> 
> The current implementation uses ilog2(SZ_64K >> PAGE_SHIFT), which
> requests 64K folios. This is optimal for 4K base pages (where CONT_PTES
> = 16, contpte size = 64K), but suboptimal for 16K and 64K base pages:
> 
> Page size | Before (order) | After (order) | contpte
> ----------|----------------|---------------|--------
> 4K        | 4 (64K)        | 4 (64K)       | Yes (unchanged)
> 16K       | 2 (64K)        | 7 (2M)        | Yes (new)
> 64K       | 0 (64K)        | 5 (2M)        | Yes (new)
> 
> For 16K pages, CONT_PTES = 128 and the contpte size is 2M (order 7).
> For 64K pages, CONT_PTES = 32 and the contpte size is 2M (order 5).
> 
> Use ilog2(CONT_PTES) instead, which directly evaluates to contpte-aligned
> order for all page sizes.
> 
> The worst-case waste is bounded to one folio (up to 2MB - 64KB)
> at the end of the file, since page_cache_ra_order() reduces the folio
> order near EOF to avoid allocating past i_size.

So, if you have a smallish text segment in a larger file, we'd always
try to allocate 2M on 16k/64k?

That feels wrong.

Asking the other way around: why not also use 2M on a 4k system and end
up with a PMD?

And no, I don't think we should default to that, just emphasizing my
point that *maybe* we really want to consider mapping (vma) size as well.

-- 
Cheers,

David

>>      exec readahead sets async_size = 0, so no PG_readahead markers
>>      are placed.
>>
>> With no decrements, mmap_miss monotonically increases past
>> MMAP_LOTSAMISS after 100 page faults, disabling all subsequent
>> exec readahead.
>>
>> Fix this by moving the VM_EXEC readahead block above the mmap_miss
>> check. The exec readahead path is targeted. It reads a single folio at
>> the fault location with async_size=0, not speculative prefetch, so the
>> mmap_miss heuristic designed to throttle wasteful speculative readahead
>> should not gate it. The page would need to be faulted in regardless,
>> the only question is at what order.
>>
>> [1] https://lore.kernel.org/all/20250430145920.3748738-6-ryan.roberts@arm.com/
>>
>> Signed-off-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>

> I can see the problem but I'm not sure what you propose is the right fix.
> If you move the VM_EXEC logic earlier, you'll effectively disable
> VM_HUGEPAGE handling for VM_EXEC vmas which I don't think we want. So
> shouldn't we rather disable mmap_miss logic for VM_EXEC vmas like:
> 
> 	if (!(vm_flags & (VM_SEQ_READ | VM_EXEC))) {
> 		...
> 	}
> 

That sounds reasonable to me.

-- 
Cheers,

David

> On Fri, 13 Mar 2026 13:55:38 -0700 Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev> wrote:

>>>

>>> that never actually gets used. And that is independent of page size.

>>> about memory pressure in their 16K config, for example.
>>>
>>
>> The force_thp_readahead path in do_sync_mmap_readahead() reads at
>> HPAGE_PMD_ORDER (2M on x86) and even doubles it to 4M for
>> non VM_RAND_READ mappings (ra->size *= 2), with async readahead
>> enabled. exec_folio_order() is more conservative. a single 2M folio
>> with async_size=0, no speculative prefetch. So I think the memory
>> pressure would not be worse than what x86 has?
>>
>> For memory pressure on Android 16K: the readahead is clamped to VMA
>> boundaries, so a small shared library won't read 2M.
>> page_cache_ra_order() reduces folio order near EOF and on allocation
>> failure, so the 2M order is a preference, not a guarantee with the
>> current code?
>>
> 
> I am not a big fan of introducing Kconfig options, but would
> CONFIG_EXEC_FOLIO_ORDER with the default value being 64K be a better
> solution? Or maybe a default of 64K for 4K and 16K base page size,
> but 2M for 64K page size as 64K base page size is mostly for servers.
> 
> Using a default value of 64K would mean no change in behaviour.

I don't think such a tunable is the right approach. We should try to do
something smarter in the kernel.

We should have access to the mapping size and whether there is currently
real memory pressure. We might even know the estimated speed of the
device we're loading data from :)

-- 
Cheers,

David
